title: NSAIDs and Paracetamol
summary: مضادات الالتهاب غير الستيرويدية (NSAIDs) تعمل عبر تثبيط إنزيمات COX-1 وCOX-2 مما يقلل تخليق البروستاغلاندينات. الأسبرين يثبط COX بشكل لا عكسي ويُستخدم بجرعات منخفضة كمضاد للتجلط. مثبطات COX-2 الانتقائية كـ Celecoxib تسبب آثاراً معدية أقل لكنها تزيد خطر الاحتشاء. الباراسيتامول مسكن ومخفض للحرارة لكنه ليس مضاداً للالتهاب.

key_drugs.name: Aspirin (Acetylsalicylic acid)
key_drugs.note: low dose: antiplatelet (75-150mg); intermediate: analgesic/antipyretic (300-650mg); high: anti-inflammatory (4-8g)

key_drugs.name: Ibuprofen
key_drugs.note: first-choice in inflammatory joint disease; best NSAID in children; less GI side effects

key_drugs.name: Diclofenac
key_drugs.note: best NSAID for dysmenorrhea; more nephrotoxic; less GI irritation than aspirin

key_drugs.name: Indomethacin
key_drugs.note: most potent but most toxic; closes patent ductus arteriosus; avoid in late pregnancy

key_drugs.name: Celecoxib
key_drugs.note: less GI ulcer risk; nephrotoxic; increases MI risk; skin rash

key_drugs.name: Paracetamol (Acetaminophen)
key_drugs.note: NO anti-inflammatory; preferred antipyretic; hepatotoxic in overdose; antidote: N-acetylcysteine

mnemonics.title: GRABS - الآثار الجانبية المشتركة للـ NSAIDs
mnemonics.body: G = GI upset and ulceration, R = Renal toxicity (nephrotoxicity), A = Allergy (skin rash, rhinitis, asthma), B = Blood pressure increase (fluid retention, edema), S = Salicylism (chronic aspirin toxicity: dizziness, tinnitus)

mnemonics.title: قصة الأسبرين مع الصفائح الدموية
mnemonics.body: تخيل الأسبرين كسكين يقطع COX للأبد (لا عكسي = Irreversible). الصفيحة الدموية لا نواة لها (anucleated) فلا تستطيع صنع COX جديداً → تأثيره يمتد لـ 7-10 أيام (عمر الصفيحة). البطانة الوعائية عندها نواة → تصنع COX جديداً بسرعة → تعود لتصنيع PGI2.

mnemonics.title: Paracetamol - الكبد في خطر
mnemonics.body: تخيل كبداً يعمل بشكل طبيعي مع الباراسيتامول. في جرعة زائدة: مسار الغلوتاثيون (Glutathione) يَنضب → NAPQI (المستقلب السام) يتراكم كالنار → نخر كبدي. الترياق N-acetylcysteine يُجدد الغلوتاثيون ويُطفئ النار.

mnemonics.title: BRING - موانع الأسبرين
mnemonics.body: B = Bleeding disorders, R = Renal problems, I = Infections viral in children (Reye's syndrome), N = NSAID allergy/GI ulcer, G = Gout (low doses increase uric acid)

mnemonics.title: Indomethacin - القوي الخطير
mnemonics.body: Indomethacin الأقوى بين الـ NSAIDs لكن أيضاً الأخطر. يُغلق القناة الشريانية (ductus arteriosus) في المواليد (استخدام قانوني) لكنه خطير في نهاية الحمل (يُغلق القناة مبكراً في الجنين). أثاره العصبية (Neurotoxicity) والدموية (aplastic anemia) تجعله للحالات المحدودة فقط.

flashcards.front: What is the main difference between aspirin's COX inhibition and that of other NSAIDs?
flashcards.back: Aspirin: irreversible inhibition by forming a covalent (acetyl) bond. Other NSAIDs: reversible, temporary inhibition lasting hours.

flashcards.front: Why does aspirin's antiplatelet effect last 7-10 days?
flashcards.back: Platelets are anucleated cells → they cannot synthesize new COX → the irreversible inhibition lasts the entire lifespan of the platelet (7-10 days).

flashcards.front: What are the different doses of aspirin and their effects?
flashcards.back: Low dose (75-150 mg/d): antiplatelet. Intermediate dose (300-650 mg/4-6h): analgesic and antipyretic. High dose (4-8 g/d): anti-inflammatory.

flashcards.front: What is the dangerous drug interaction of aspirin with warfarin?
flashcards.back: Salicylates bind strongly to albumin → they displace warfarin → free warfarin in the blood rises → increased risk of bleeding.

flashcards.front: What is Reye's syndrome and when does it occur?
flashcards.back: A serious encephalopathy and liver damage in children who take aspirin during a viral infection (such as influenza or chickenpox). Therefore aspirin is contraindicated in children under 12 years.

flashcards.front: What is the difference in the effect on uric acid between low and high doses of aspirin?
flashcards.back: Low dose: competes with uric acid for renal secretion → reduces its excretion → raises its blood level (hyperuricemia → gout). High dose: increases uric acid excretion (uricosuric effect).

flashcards.front: How is acute aspirin poisoning treated?
flashcards.back: Gastric lavage + alkalinization of urine with NaHCO3 → increases ionization of salicylate → enhances renal excretion → accelerates drug elimination.

flashcards.front: What is the best NSAID for dysmenorrhea?
flashcards.back: Diclofenac is better than ibuprofen and indomethacin in treating dysmenorrhea.

flashcards.front: What is the unique use of indomethacin in newborns?
flashcards.back: It closes the patent ductus arteriosus in newborns by inhibiting the prostaglandins that keep it open.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of celecoxib over non-selective NSAIDs?
flashcards.back: It selectively inhibits COX-2 without COX-1 → it does not inhibit the gastroprotective PGs → lower risk of gastric ulcer and GI bleeding.

flashcards.front: What are the serious adverse effects specific to celecoxib?
flashcards.back: 1. Nephrotoxicity. 2. Skin rash. 3. Increased risk of myocardial infarction (MI) due to inhibition of PGI2 without inhibition of TXA2.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of action of paracetamol and where does it act?
flashcards.back: Its exact mechanism is uncertain, but it acts centrally (in the brain) to relieve pain and fever. It does not effectively inhibit COX in peripheral tissues → no anti-inflammatory effect.

flashcards.front: Why does paracetamol overdose cause liver damage?
flashcards.back: At normal doses paracetamol is metabolized safely. In overdose: the glutathione pathway is depleted → NAPQI (a toxic metabolite) accumulates → hepatic necrosis within 72 hours.

flashcards.front: What is the antidote for paracetamol poisoning and why?
flashcards.back: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) replenishes the hepatic glutathione stores → neutralizes the toxic NAPQI → protects the liver from necrosis. It must be given early.

flashcards.front: In which situations is paracetamol preferred over aspirin as an antipyretic?
flashcards.back: 1. Children (risk of Reye's syndrome). 2. Peptic ulcer. 3. Bleeding disorders. 4. Hyperuricemia (gout). 5. Pregnancy. 6. Aspirin allergy.

flashcards.front: Which NSAID is the best tolerated (fewest side effects) and which is the most toxic?
flashcards.back: Best tolerated: ibuprofen (fewest side effects, used in children). Most toxic: indomethacin (neurotoxicity, aplastic anemia, used in limited cases only).

flashcards.front: What is the general mechanism of NSAIDs?
flashcards.back: Inhibition of the COX enzyme → decreased prostaglandin synthesis → analgesic, antipyretic, and anti-inflammatory effects.

flashcards.front: Why is aspirin given at a low dose for cardiac prophylaxis?
flashcards.back: It irreversibly inhibits COX-1 in platelets → reduces TXA2 → antiplatelet effect.

flashcards.front: What are the renal effects of NSAIDs?
flashcards.back: Decreased renal prostaglandins → decreased perfusion → sodium and water retention and renal injury.

flashcards.front: What is an NSAID ulcer?
flashcards.back: Inhibition of gastric COX-1 → decreased mucosal protection → ulcer and GI bleeding.

flashcards.front: What is paracetamol and its mechanism?
flashcards.back: A central analgesic and antipyretic; weak as an anti-inflammatory; its overdose is hepatotoxic (antidote NAC).

flashcards.front: What is the risk of celecoxib?
flashcards.back: Selective COX-2 → less ulceration but higher cardiovascular risk.

flashcards.front: What is Reye's syndrome?
flashcards.back: Hepatic encephalopathy when aspirin is given to children with a viral infection → contraindicated.

flashcards.front: Why are NSAIDs contraindicated in the last trimester of pregnancy?
flashcards.back: They may close the ductus arteriosus prematurely.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between aspirin and other NSAIDs in COX inhibition?
flashcards.back: Aspirin inhibits COX irreversibly (covalently); the rest are reversible inhibitors.

mcqs.q: A patient takes low-dose aspirin (81 mg/day). What is the primary therapeutic goal?
mcqs.explanation: Low-dose aspirin (75–150 mg/d) irreversibly inhibits TXA2 in platelets → prevents platelet aggregation. Higher doses are needed for analgesia, antipyresis, or anti-inflammation.

mcqs.q: Why is aspirin contraindicated in a child with influenza?
options: It raises blood pressure in children
mcqs.explanation: Aspirin given during viral infections in children under 12 years is associated with Reye's syndrome: a serious encephalopathy with hepatic damage. Paracetamol is used instead.

mcqs.q: Which of the following drugs closes the patent ductus arteriosus in newborns?
mcqs.explanation: Indomethacin is used to close the patent ductus arteriosus in neonates by inhibiting the prostaglandins that keep it open. It must be avoided in late pregnancy for the same reason.

mcqs.q: A patient with a peptic ulcer needs an anti-inflammatory drug for arthritis. Which is most appropriate?
mcqs.explanation: Celecoxib selectively inhibits COX-2 without inhibiting COX-1 → does not remove gastric mucosal protection → lower ulcer risk. The optimal choice for a patient with peptic ulcer who needs an anti-inflammatory.

mcqs.q: Which adverse effect is specific to Celecoxib and not seen with non-selective NSAIDs?
options: Increased risk of myocardial infarction + skin rash
mcqs.explanation: Celecoxib inhibits PGI2 (vasodilator + antiplatelet) without inhibiting TXA2 → imbalance → prothrombotic state → increased MI risk. Skin rash is also specific to Celecoxib.

mcqs.q: A patient took a paracetamol overdose 2 hours ago. What is the most appropriate antidote?
mcqs.explanation: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) replenishes hepatic glutathione → neutralizes the toxic NAPQI metabolite → prevents hepatic necrosis. It should be given early (within 8–10 hours of ingestion) for best results.

mcqs.q: Which NSAID is the best choice for dysmenorrhea?
mcqs.explanation: Diclofenac is superior to ibuprofen and indomethacin for dysmenorrhea due to its high efficacy and reasonable side-effect profile.

mcqs.q: Why is gastric lavage and urinary alkalinization used in aspirin poisoning?
options: Because urinary alkalinization increases salicylate ionization and accelerates renal excretion
mcqs.explanation: Urinary alkalinization with NaHCO3 raises urine pH → increases ionization of salicylate → ionized drug cannot be reabsorbed in renal tubules → excreted in urine → faster elimination.

mcqs.q: A patient on low-dose aspirin develops joint pain. Why not simply increase the aspirin dose for anti-inflammatory effect?
options: High doses abolish the antiplatelet effect and increase thrombotic risk
Aspirin converts to a different drug at high doses
High doses cause uric acid deficiency
mcqs.explanation: High-dose aspirin also inhibits vascular PGI2 (in addition to platelet TXA2) → imbalance → loss of antithrombotic effect. It also increases bleeding risk and other side effects.

mcqs.q: Which drug is best for fever reduction in a 5-year-old child with a viral illness?
mcqs.explanation: Aspirin is contraindicated under 12 years (Reye's syndrome risk). Ibuprofen is the best NSAID for children; paracetamol is safe and effective as an antipyretic. Either is appropriate.

mcqs.q: Aspirin is contraindicated in children because of the risk of:
mcqs.explanation: Reye's syndrome is a serious hepatic encephalopathy triggered by aspirin during viral infections in children.

mcqs.q: Compared to non-selective NSAIDs, Celecoxib has:
options: Less GI ulceration but higher cardiovascular risk
mcqs.explanation: Celecoxib selectively inhibits COX-2, sparing gastric COX-1, but increases thrombotic/cardiovascular risk.

high_yield.point: Aspirin يثبّط COX بشكل لا رجعي → تأثير مضاد للصفائح يدوم عمر الصفيحة (7–10 أيام).
Celecoxib انتقائي COX-2 → أقل أذية معدية لكن خطر قلبي وعائي أعلى.
Paracetamol مسكّن/خافض حرارة لكنه ضعيف كمضاد التهاب (لا يثبّط COX طرفياً يُذكر) ولا يسبب أذية معدية.
Aspirin يُمنع في الأطفال (متلازمة Reye مع العدوى الفيروسية).
آثار NSAIDs: قرحة معدية، أذية كلوية، احتباس سوائل، تثبيط الصفائح.

comparison_tables.title: Non-selective vs COX-2 selective vs Paracetamol
